metadata.title: Why the Agentic Loop Can Delete Your Files Before You Finish Reading the Plan

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: The unsettling part is that the explanation and the file changes happen inside the same loop, so a file is modified before I finish reading the plan. I want plan mode to be automatic before any build. For adding a contact page to ~/class-website: (1) show me exactly when to press Shift+Tab twice and what read-only mode still lets Claude do; (2) walk me through reviewing the proposed plan before Act begins; (3) tell me how I confirm nothing was edited or installed while I was reading.

props.command: The unsettling part is that the explanation and the file changes happen inside the same loop, so a file is modified before I finish reading the plan. I want plan mode to be automatic before any build. For adding a contact page to ~/class-website: (1) show me exactly when to press Shift+Tab twice and what read-only mode still lets Claude do; (2) walk me through reviewing the proposed plan before Act begins; (3) tell me how I confirm nothing was edited or installed while I was reading.
props.segment: Why the Agentic Loop Can Delete

beats.narration_text: The teacher types a request. Claude starts writing. A file is already modified before the teacher finishes reading the first sentence.

card.copy: Why the agentic loop acts before you finish reading

beats.new_visual_element: [TITLE] the loop acts before you read

beats.narration_text: A chatbot produces text. You read it. You decide. Nothing happens until you act on it. Claude.ai works this way. Claude Code does not.

production_viz.label: chatbot: text you review vs. agent: actions that execute
production_viz.mechanic: two side-by-side columns; left 'Claude.ai (chatbot)' shows text flowing to a review step that the user controls; right 'Claude Code (agent)' shows a looping cycle with 'Gather / Act / Verify' that runs without pause

beats.narration_text: The agentic loop: Claude gathers context — reads your files, checks the project state. Then acts: edits a file, runs a command, calls an API. Then verifies — checks what happened. And loops again. Many iterations can run before you see any output.

production_viz.label: the three-node loop: Gather, Act, Verify
production_viz.mechanic: three nodes in a cycle — 'Gather' (ink), 'Act' (crimson, blinking), 'Verify' (ink) — with arrows between them; the Act node has a file-icon label showing a file being modified in real time

beats.narration_text: The teacher opens terminal in ~/class-website. Types: Add a contact page. Claude reads index.html. Proposes a plan. Then immediately begins writing contact.html, modifying nav in index.html, and installing a form library — all before the teacher reads the output.
beats.scene_description: a terminal window pinned to newsprint showing Claude Code output with three file operations executing in sequence, editorial collage style, desaturated
beats.image_prompt: B04, printed terminal output showing three file operations executing in sequence — contact.html written, nav modified, npm install running — before any review is possible, pinned to aged newsprint, editorial collage, highly desaturated, cream and charcoal tones
beats.new_visual_element: [STILL ai] terminal showing loop executing before user reads

beats.narration_text: Here is the question. A chatbot should produce text the user reviews before anything changes. Here is the system where file changes happen inside the same loop as the explanation. Why?

card.copy: Files changed while the explanation was still scrolling. Why?

beats.narration_text: Claude Code is an agentic system. The loop is the product. Gather, act, verify, repeat — the loop can complete three, four, five steps before the output buffer flushes to your terminal. The explanation and the file changes are happening at the same time, in the same loop, because that is the design. The loop is what makes Claude Code useful for complex multi-step work.

production_viz.label: the loop and the output buffer are asynchronous
production_viz.mechanic: the three-node loop runs on one side of the frame while a terminal output buffer fills character by character on the other; file-change icons appear in the loop before the buffer completes; a serif label 'explanation and actions share the same loop'

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] loop running async with output buffer

beats.narration_text: Plan mode is the interruption point. Shift+Tab twice. Claude enters read-only mode. It can read files, propose a plan, explain what it would do — but it cannot edit, run, or change anything. The loop stops at the Gather step. You review the plan before Act begins.

production_viz.label: plan mode: a gate between Gather and Act
production_viz.mechanic: the three-node loop again; a teal gate appears between 'Gather' and 'Act'; the gate is labeled 'PLAN MODE'; the loop can only pass the gate when the user approves; the Act node is grayed until approval

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] plan mode gate between gather and act

beats.narration_text: The same request — Add a contact page — in plan mode. Claude reads the project, proposes: create contact.html here, add a nav entry here, no new dependencies. The plan is on screen. The teacher reads it. Edits it: no form library, use the existing styles only. Then approves.

production_viz.label: the plan visible before execution
production_viz.mechanic: a plan card with three steps — 'create contact.html', 'add nav entry', 'install form library' — the third step is struck through by the teacher's edit; a teal approved chip appears; only then does the loop continue to Act

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] plan reviewed and edited before approval

beats.narration_text: Here is where this bites. The loop's autonomy is what makes Claude Code powerful for multi-step classroom builds — generating a grading tool, building a simulation, building three pages at once. But a loop that runs unsupervised on a project you care about can modify things you did not intend to touch, install dependencies your school server cannot serve, change a config that breaks a different project.

production_viz.label: same loop — supervised vs. unsupervised
production_viz.mechanic: two outcome paths from the loop: left 'with plan mode' shows a controlled chain of file changes, each with a teal checkmark; right 'without plan mode' shows the same changes but with a crimson surprise addition — a dependency install that fails on the school server

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] two outcomes: plan mode vs. no plan mode

beats.narration_text: The practical move: use plan mode for any first session on a project and for any operation whose failure would cost more than 30 seconds to undo. Press Shift+Tab twice. Read the plan. Use Ctrl+G to open it in your editor and remove what you did not ask for. Then approve. The loop runs only what you approved.

production_viz.label: the heuristic: plan mode for anything costly to undo
production_viz.mechanic: a two-row table: row 1 'first session on any project -> plan mode'; row 2 'operation that is costly to undo -> plan mode'; a teal rule under each row

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] two-row heuristic table for when to use plan mode

beats.narration_text: The agentic loop is what makes Claude Code more than a better chatbot. The loop reads your project, executes a plan across many files, verifies the result — and does it without you managing each step. Plan mode is not overhead. It is the dial that lets you choose how much of the loop to supervise on any given task.

production_viz.label: plan mode as a supervision dial
production_viz.mechanic: a dial labeled 'supervision' with two positions: 'plan mode on' (left, teal) and 'plan mode off' (right, ink); a note shows the loop capability is the same; only the interruption point changes

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] supervision dial showing plan mode as a toggle

beats.narration_text: The agentic loop acts before you finish reading because that is what makes it useful. Plan mode exists because useful is not the same as supervised. Shift+Tab twice, read the plan, edit what does not belong. Then let the loop run.

card.copy: The loop acts. Plan mode lets you read first.

beats.new_visual_element: [CARD] endcard — loop acts; plan mode lets you read first

beats.title: Liam verdict — ClaudeVerdictArtifact
beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Here's what the body just demonstrated. The agentic loop is what makes Claude Code more than a better chatbot. The loop reads your project, executes a plan across many files, verifies the result — and does it without you managing each step. Plan mode is not overhead. It is the dial that lets you choose how much of the loop to supervise on any given task. The agentic loop acts before you finish reading because that is what makes it useful. Plan mode exists because useful is not the same as supervised. Shift+Tab twice, read the plan, edit what does not belong. Then let the loop run.

beats.title: Your turn handoff — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Take this prompt, run it on your own — pick any cancer type or clinical scenario you know about and ask how this mechanism applies there.

props.command: Explain how [Why the Agentic Loop Can Delete Your Files Before You Finish Reading t] applies to a specific cancer type or clinical case you're studying. What proteins are involved, what goes wrong at the molecular level, and what is the therapeutic or diagnostic significance?
